commands.description: Initialize a local Git repository in the current directory.
commands.mistake: Running git init inside a folder that is already inside an existing Git repository, creating nested repository states.

commands.description: Clone a remote repository to your local system.
commands.mistake: Forgetting to cd into the newly cloned directory after cloning, and running commands in the parent folder.

commands.description: Set repository or global configuration values like username and email.
commands.syntax:
git config --global user.name "Name"
git config --global user.email "email@domain.com"
commands.example:
git config --global user.name "Jane Doe"
git config --global user.email "jane@example.com"
commands.mistake: Setting configurations locally in a directory not initialized as a git repository, or typing typos in emails leading to untracked GitHub stats.

commands.description: Show the status of files in the working directory and staging area (tracked/untracked/modified).
commands.mistake: Relying on status blindly before committing without double checking the actual file diffs.

commands.description: Add file changes in your working directory to the staging area.
commands.syntax:
git add <file-or-directory>
# Stage all changes:
git add .
commands.mistake: Staging secret credential files, environment files (.env), or node_modules accidentally using 'git add .'.

commands.description: Record staged snapshots in version history.
commands.example: git commit -m "feat: implement client-side regex tester"
commands.mistake: Writing generic commit messages like 'fix' or 'update' which make history hard to search, or committing without staging first.

commands.description: Show commit logs/history of the active branch.
commands.mistake: Forgetting that git log shows history page-by-page and getting stuck inside the command pager (press 'q' to exit).

commands.description: Show changes between commits, commit and working tree, or staged and unstaged states.
commands.syntax:
git diff [commit-hash]
# Show staged differences:
git diff --staged
commands.mistake: Running git diff without staging parameters and wondering why staged changes are not showing up.

commands.description: Show various types of objects (commits, tags, files) with their content/diff metadata.
commands.mistake: Running git show without a commit hash when you want to view a past commit, which default shows only the latest commit.

commands.description: Remove files from the working tree and index (stage).
commands.syntax:
git rm <filepath>
# Untrack file but keep it locally:
git rm --cached <filepath>
commands.mistake: Running 'git rm <file>' without '--cached' when you want to keep the local copy; this will permanently delete the local file.

commands.description: Move or rename a file, a directory, or a symlink.
commands.mistake: Renaming files directly using your OS explorer; git treats it as deleted and untracked. Use git mv to preserve file history.

commands.description: Display help information/manual pages for Git commands.
commands.mistake: Searching online manuals when git help runs the complete offline documentation instantly in your local browser.

commands.description: Switch branches or restore working tree files.
commands.syntax:
git checkout <branch-name>
# Switch to past commit:
git checkout <commit-hash>
commands.mistake: Switching branches with uncommitted modifications, which can cause conflict blockages.

commands.description: Switch branches safely (a cleaner alternative to git checkout for branch navigation).
commands.syntax:
git switch <branch-name>
# Create and switch:
git switch -c <branch-name>
commands.mistake: Forgetting that git switch only works for branches, not for viewing specific commits or file resets (use restore for files).

commands.description: Restore working tree files from index or specific commits.
commands.syntax:
git restore <filepath>
# Unstage file changes:
git restore --staged <filepath>
commands.mistake: Discarding modifications to working files; restored modifications cannot be recovered.

commands.description: List, create, or delete branches.
commands.syntax:
git branch # list local
git branch -a # list local + remote
git branch -d <branch-name> # delete merged branch
git branch -D <branch-name> # force delete branch
commands.mistake: Trying to delete the branch you are currently on. Switch to another branch first.

commands.description: Merge target branch history into the active head branch.
commands.mistake: Merging without running git pull first, leading to conflicts on outdated commits.

commands.description: Reapply commits on top of another base tip. Keeps a clean linear history.
commands.mistake: Rebasing commits that have already been pushed to public/shared branches, rewriting history for other developers.

commands.description: Apply the changes introduced by some existing commits onto the current branch.
commands.mistake: Cherry-picking commits without realizing they depend on changes introduced in prior commits, causing build failures.

commands.description: Find as good common ancestors as possible for a merge.
commands.mistake: Using manual diff comparisons when merge-base identifies exactly where the branch diverged.

commands.description: Summarize git log output, grouping commits by author.
commands.mistake: Running shortlog without tags in huge repositories, resulting in spamming logs for thousands of commits.

commands.description: Manage set of tracked repositories (remotes).
commands.syntax:
git remote -v
git remote add <name> <url>
git remote remove <name>
commands.mistake: Adding duplicate remote names or typos in URLs, making it impossible to pull/push.

commands.description: Download objects and refs from another repository. Does not modify local files.
commands.mistake: Thinking git fetch updates your active working code. You must merge or rebase after fetching to sync.

commands.description: Fetch from and integrate with another repository or local branch.
commands.mistake: Running git pull when you have dirty uncommitted local modifications, causing automatic merging to fail.

commands.description: Update remote refs along with associated objects.
commands.syntax:
git push <remote> <branch>
# Set upstream tracking branch:
git push -u origin <branch>
commands.mistake: Using push --force blindly on master/main branches, destroying other team members' code snapshots.

commands.description: Delete stale tracking branches under <name> that no longer exist on the remote.
commands.mistake: Expecting local physical branches to be deleted. It only cleans obsolete remote-tracking markers (e.g. origin/feature-x).

commands.description: Give information about a specific remote repository.
commands.mistake: Running this offline; it requires active remote network communication to check branch sync states.

commands.description: Clone repository containing all branch references, tags, and settings as a full mirror.
commands.mistake: Cloning a mirror expecting a normal working tree; it contains only raw .git databases (bare repository).

commands.description: Initialize, update, or inspect nested submodules inside a Git repository.
commands.syntax:
git submodule add <url> <path>
git submodule update --init --recursive
commands.mistake: Cloning a project containing submodules without running 'submodule update --init', leaving submodules empty.

commands.description: Generate a summary of pending changes to ask upstream maintainers to pull.
commands.mistake: Specifying wrong range boundaries, causing unnecessary or unrelated changes to be requested.

commands.description: Undo the last commit(s), moving HEAD back, but keeping all changes staged in the index.
commands.mistake: Assuming changes are deleted. This command preserves all modified code in your staged index.

commands.description: Undo commits and unstage changes, leaving files modified in working directory. This is the default reset mode.
commands.mistake: Running this when you wanted to keep modifications staged. You will have to re-add changes to the stage.

commands.description: Discard all uncommitted changes and revert to a specific commit. Warning: destructive operation!
commands.mistake: Executing reset --hard when you have valuable uncommitted modifications; they will be permanently lost.

commands.description: Create a new commit that undoes the effects of previous commits without rewriting history.
commands.mistake: Reverting a commit that was already reverted, causing confusing logical code loops in history.

commands.description: Show a record of all commits, branch switches, resets, and merges. Best tool to recover 'lost' commits.
commands.mistake: Assuming deleted branches or hard resets are unrecoverable without checking reflog first.

commands.description: Remove untracked files and directories from the working tree.
commands.syntax:
git clean -fd
# Dry run check first:
git clean -nfd
commands.mistake: Running clean without double-checking the files list via dry-run (-n), which permanently deletes local configuration files.

commands.description: Replace the tip commit of the current branch with a new commit, allowing changes or rewrites to message.
commands.mistake: Amending a commit that has already been pushed to a shared remote, which will require force-pushing.

commands.description: Discard local changes in the working directory (older equivalent to git restore).
commands.mistake: Conflating this branch-switching command syntax, which is why git restore is preferred in modern Git.

commands.description: Temporarily save (stash) changes in your dirty working directory so you can work on something else.
commands.syntax:
git stash save "<message>"
# Save with untracked files:
git stash -u
commands.mistake: Stashing without keeping track of what was modified, then accumulating 30 stashes and forgetting which is which.

commands.description: List stashed state changes.
commands.mistake: Stashing changes and then forgetting about them, resulting in merge issues weeks later.

commands.description: Remove the single most recently stashed state from the stash list and apply it to active branch.
commands.mistake: Popping a stash on a branch different from where you created it, causing messy merge conflicts.

commands.description: Apply stashed changes to active branch, but do not delete the stash from the stashing list.
commands.mistake: Applying stash multiple times by mistake, creating duplicated merge conflict sections.

commands.description: Remove a single stashed state from the stash list.
commands.mistake: Dropping the wrong stash index because the indices shift after popping or dropping other items.

commands.description: Remove all stashed states. Warning: irreversible action!
commands.mistake: Clearing the entire stash pool when you wanted to delete a single stash index, resulting in lost stashed code.

commands.description: Show the changes recorded in the stash as a diff.
commands.mistake: Running stash show without '-p' when you want to view the actual file diffs (defaults to file stats summary).

commands.description: List tags in alphabetical order, or look up specific tags.
commands.mistake: Forgetting that git tag only lists local tags unless updated via git fetch.

commands.description: Create an annotated tag containing tagger metadata, date, and messages, GPG signed if specified.
commands.example: git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release v1.0.0 stable release version"
commands.mistake: Creating lightweight tags (without -a) for public releases, which omit tagger identification details.

commands.description: Delete a local tag.
commands.mistake: Deleting a local tag thinking it automatically deletes the tag on GitHub. Remote tags must be purged separately.

commands.description: Push all local tags that are not yet on the remote server.
commands.mistake: Running 'git push' without '--tags' when publishing releases; normal push commands do not transfer tags.

commands.description: Delete a tag on the remote repository.
commands.syntax:
git push <remote-name> --delete <tag-name>
# Older syntax:
git push <remote-name> :refs/tags/<tag-name>
commands.mistake: Leaving a broken tag on remote repositories which causes conflict failures for other developers during fetch.

commands.description: Start an interactive rebase session to edit, squash, reword, or drop commits in history.
commands.mistake: Closing the text editor without realizing it initiates the rebase, or deleting commits accidentally from the pick list.

commands.description: Manage multiple working trees attached to the same repository. Work on multiple branches in different folders simultaneously.
commands.syntax:
git worktree add <path> <branch>
git worktree list
git worktree remove <path>
commands.mistake: Modifying files inside a worktree thinking it is a separate repository, and forgetting to prune removed worktrees.

commands.description: Use binary search to find the commit that introduced a bug.
commands.syntax:
git bisect start
git bisect bad # Current version has the bug
git bisect good <commit-hash> # Last known working version
# Type 'git bisect good' or 'git bisect bad' iteratively, then:
git bisect reset
commands.example:
git bisect start
git bisect bad
git bisect good v1.0.0
commands.mistake: Forgetting to run 'git bisect reset' after finding the bug, leaving the repository in a detached HEAD bisect state.

commands.description: Show what revision and author last modified each line of a file.
commands.mistake: Blaming files that have been auto-formatted, which shows the formatter author instead of the logic author (use --ignore-revs-file if needed).

commands.description: Create a zip or tar archive of the files in a repository from specific branches or commits.
commands.mistake: Archiving repositories and expecting the .git history directory to be included; it only packs working directory code.

commands.description: Run garbage collection to optimize database size, clean up unreachable commits, and compress history.
commands.mistake: Pruning reflog commits that you might still want to restore. Use with caution!

commands.description: Verify the connectivity and validity of objects in the database, checking for corrupted commits.
commands.mistake: Running fsck and deleting loose objects without back-ups if corruption occurred.

commands.description: Prepare commits for email submission, generating a series of .patch files.
commands.mistake: Creating format-patches in a folder where you have local uncommitted changes, making organization messy.

commands.description: Apply a series of patches from a mailbox/local patch files.
commands.mistake: Applying patches generated from an incompatible branch state, leading to patch conflict failures.

commands.description: Package objects and references in an offline file. Useful for sharing code without remote servers.
commands.syntax:
git bundle create <filename> <branch-name>
# Clone from a bundle:
git clone <filename> -b <branch>
commands.mistake: Forgetting to specify references (branches/tags) when packaging, resulting in an empty bundle file.

commands.description: Find the most recent tag reachable from a commit, suffixing it with commit counts and hashes.
commands.mistake: Assuming it works in a repository without any tags, which will fall back to returning only the commit hash.

commands.description: You accidentally committed code directly to the 'main' branch instead of a dedicated feature branch.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Create a new branch pointing to current changes:
git branch feat/your-feature-name
# 2. Reset local main back one commit (or more):
git reset --hard HEAD~1
# 3. Switch to your feature branch:
git checkout feat/your-feature-name
commands.example:
git branch feat/search-bar
git reset --hard HEAD~1
git checkout feat/search-bar
commands.mistake: Performing this action if you already pushed main to GitHub. You will have to coordinate with your team to force-push.

commands.description: You made a commit but immediately realized you made a typo or forgot to include a file, and want to redo it.
commands.syntax:
# Undo commit, keep files modified and staged:
git reset --soft HEAD~1
# Undo commit, keep files modified but unstaged:
git reset HEAD~1
commands.mistake: Using 'git reset --hard HEAD~1' instead, which will completely delete the changes you just made.

commands.description: You accidentally ran 'git reset --hard' and lost all your uncommitted or committed work.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Run reflog to find the commit hash before the reset:
git reflog
# 2. Revert HEAD to that commit hash:
git reset --hard <commit-hash>
commands.example:
git reflog
# Output showing: HEAD@{1}: reset: moving to HEAD~1
# HEAD@{2}: commit: feat: added sidebar
git reset --hard a2f3c9e
commands.mistake: Running 'git clean -fd' or closing the workspace before fetching the hash from the log, or thinking uncommitted changes can be recovered this way (reflog only recovers committed changes).

commands.description: You accidentally committed a file containing API keys, passwords, or secrets, and need to scrub it from all branch histories.
commands.syntax:
# Remove the file from all commits and rewrite history:
git filter-branch --force --index-filter \
  "git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch <filename>" \
  --prune-empty --tag-name-filter cat -- --all
# Note: Force pushing is required afterwards:
git push origin --force --all
commands.mistake: Assuming simple 'git rm file' deletes it from history. The keys will still be accessible in past commits unless history is rewritten.

commands.description: You changed a filename's casing (e.g., header.tsx to Header.tsx), but Git doesn't detect the modification due to default config.
commands.syntax:
# Force rename via Git:
git mv <old-name> <new-name>
# Alternatively, disable core ignorecase:
git config core.ignorecase false
commands.mistake: Renaming the file directly in your OS Explorer or VS Code sidebar. Git will show duplicate untracked/deleted files.

commands.description: You need to rename your active local branch and update the remote tracking branch accordingly.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Rename active branch locally:
git branch -m <new-name>
# 2. Push the new branch and set remote upstream:
git push origin -u <new-name>
# 3. Delete the old branch on the remote server:
git push origin --delete <old-name>
commands.example:
git branch -m feat/search-ui
git push origin -u feat/search-ui
git push origin --delete feat/srch-ui
commands.mistake: Renaming a branch other developers are actively basing their pull requests on, breaking their target remotes.

commands.description: You want to completely erase all local modifications (tracked and untracked) to start over from fresh pull state.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Reset all tracked files to current commit:
git reset --hard HEAD
# 2. Remove all untracked files and directories:
git clean -fd
commands.mistake: Running 'git clean -fdx' if you want to keep files in your gitignore, like local .env files or configuration parameters.

commands.description: You committed code but realized it has the wrong git author email or name.
commands.syntax:
# Update only the last commit:
git commit --amend --author="Name <email@example.com>"
commands.mistake: Amending a commit that you have already pushed to a shared remote, which requires force-pushing.

commands.description: Your branch conflicts with another branch and Git halts the merge, inserting conflict markers.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Run status to list conflicting files:
git status
# 2. Open files, resolve markers (<<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>>), then stage:
git add <resolved-file>
# 3. Complete the merge transaction:
git commit -m "merge: resolved conflicts with origin/main"
commands.example:
git status
# resolve conflicts in files...
git add src/components/Header.tsx
git commit -m "merge: resolved conflicts"
commands.mistake: Running git commit before resolving all files, or committing code with raw conflict symbols (<<<<<<<) still left in files.

commands.description: You merged a branch but realized it broke the project, and you want to completely rollback the merge commit.
commands.syntax:
# Revert a merge commit, specifying the mainline parent index (usually 1):
git revert -m 1 <merge-commit-hash>
commands.mistake: Using normal git revert without the -m option, which causes git to throw an error since merge commits have multiple parents.

commands.description: Your push is rejected because the remote contains commits you don't have locally.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Fetch remote updates and rebase your local commits on top:
git pull --rebase origin <branch-name>
# 2. Resolve conflicts if any, and continue rebase:
git rebase --continue
# 3. Safely push modifications:
git push origin <branch-name>
commands.example: git pull --rebase origin main && git push origin main
commands.mistake: Using 'git push --force' without checking remote differences, which will overwrite other developers' work permanently.

commands.description: You deleted a branch locally using git branch -D and now want to restore it.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Find the tip commit hash of the deleted branch in reflog:
git reflog
# 2. Re-create the branch from that commit hash:
git branch <branch-name> <commit-hash>
commands.example:
git reflog
# HEAD@{3}: checkout: moving from feature-auth to main
# HEAD@{4}: commit: feat: added bcrypt auth
git branch feature-auth a1c2b3d
commands.mistake: Thinking deleted branches are gone forever, or running garbage collection (git gc) which purges dangling commit objects.

commands.description: Git commands fail with 'Another git process seems to be running in this repository' error because of a stale lock file.
commands.syntax:
# Remove the stale index lock file manually:
rm -f .git/index.lock
commands.mistake: Deleting files under the refs directory or deleting index itself, which corrupts version staging tables.

commands.description: GitHub rejects your push because you committed a file exceeding the 100MB limit.
commands.syntax:
# 1. Remove large file from active index:
git rm --cached <path-to-large-file>
# 2. Rewrite history to remove files from past commits:
git filter-branch --force --index-filter \
  "git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch <path-to-large-file>" \
  --prune-empty --tag-name-filter cat -- --all
# 3. Push history clean:
git push origin main
commands.example: git rm --cached data/large_dataset.zip && git filter-branch --force --index-filter "git rm --cached --ignore-unmatch data/large_dataset.zip" --prune-empty --tag-name-filter cat -- --all
commands.mistake: Deleting the file locally and committing the delete; the file will still exist in historical commit objects, blocking pushes.
